title: Cedric Simms v The Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands (CL G-CL 5/2022) [2026] TCASC 16 (27 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT ACTION NO. CL G-CL 5/22
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
CEDRIC SIMMS
PLAINTIFF
AND
THE ATTORNEY GENERAL OF THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
DEFENDANT
__________________________________________________________________
DECISION
__________________________________________________________________
Before His Lordship Justice Chris Selochan.
Appearances: Mr. Noel Skippings for the Plaintiff
Ms. Clemar Hippolyte and Ms. Khadija McFarlane for the
Defendant
1

Hearing Dates: 6th and 7th November 2025
Venue: Court Room #1, Supreme Court, Providenciales and virtually from the
Supreme Court, Grand Turk
Handed Down: 17th December, 2025
BACKGROUND
1. By Statement of Claim filed on 24th October, 2022, the Plaintiff seeks Damages (including
Aggravated Damages) for False Arrest, False Imprisonment and Assault and Battery as
well as costs.
2. The Plaintiff’s case is that he was unlawfully arrested by the police in Grand Turk, Turks
and Caicos Islands for breach of an Interim Protection Order contrary to section 16 of the
Domestic Violence Act CAP 11:03 (“the Domestic Violence Act”). He contends that his
arrest was unlawful because it failed to fulfil the requirements of section 22(3) of the
Domestic Violence Act CAP in that, prior to effecting the arrest, the police were not in
possession of a sworn affidavit.
3. The Plaintiff contends that following his arrest he was detained at the police station from
4th August 2022 to 10th August 2022, a period which he submits amounts to false
imprisonment. In the alternative, he claims that he was unlawfully detained from 6th
August 2022 to 10th August 2022 since, pursuant to section 22(6)(b)(i) of the Domestic
Violence Act, he should not be detained for a period of in excess of forty-eight (48) hours
before being brought before a magistrate or Justice of the Peace.
4. The Plaintiff claims that at the police station, he was placed in handcuffs and leg shackles,
including on at least two occasions during visits by his attorney-at-law. He also claims to
have suffered mental, physical and emotional pain and anguish by having to remain in
2

‘the dank, dirty, smelly prisoners’ cell’ (to use the words of the Plaintiff’s attorney-at-law
in his closing submission) at the police station.
5. In its Defence dated 2nd August 2024, the Defendant acknowledges that the Plaintiff was
indeed arrested on 4th August 2022 in Grand Turk for breach of an Interim Protection
Order. However, the Defendant contends that the arrest was lawful since the police had
reasonable grounds to suspect that the Plaintiff was in breach of an Interim Protection
Order, and that belief was based on a report from the Plaintiff’s wife that the Plaintiff had
intimidated, harassed and come within 100 feet of her at her workplace and threatened
physical violence towards her contrary to the terms of the Protection Order. The
Defendant admits that at the time of the arrest the police officers had not obtained the
requisite affidavit from the victim but contends that they had reasonable grounds to
believe that the Plaintiff had breached the Protection Order based on the information and
circumstances available to them at the time, and that on the following day (being 5th
August 2022) the Plaintiff’s wife signed a written statement evidencing the breach of the
Interim Protection Order.
6. In respect of the Plaintiff’s detention, the Defendant contends that on Friday 5th August
2022, the Plaintiff was taken to the Grand Turk Magistrate’s Court, but the court was not
sitting on that day. As such, the first available court date after the expiry of the 48-hour
period was on Monday 8th August 2022 and that, at best, any unlawful imprisonment was
for a period of two days from 8th August 2022 to 10th August 2022.
7. The Defendant admits that the Plaintiff was placed in handcuffs and shackles but
contends that this was done based on a reasonable assessment of the situation and the
perceived risk posed by the Plaintiff. According to the Defendant, these actions did not
involve excessive force and were necessary and proportionate in order to ensure the
Plaintiff’s compliance, prevent escape and to protect the officers, the Plaintiff and others,
and was only done as a security measure when the Plaintiff was taken out of the cell. The
Defendant contends that the Plaintiff was known to the law, had previously been arrested
and was considered to be at a high risk of escaping custody.
3

8. In respect of the prison conditions, the Defendant contends that the conditions of the
Plaintiff’s detention were humane and lawful and that:
a. The Plaintiff was the only person in the cell and had access to a shower and toilet
facilities while in custody.
b. The Plaintiff did in fact take showers.
c. The Plaintiff, on his request, was taken out of the cell for smoking breaks.
d. The Plaintiff was offered meals regularly.
e. The Plaintiff was given medication and escorted to the hospital to receive medical care
when requested.
f. The police checked on the Plaintiff regularly to ensure that he was in good health.
g. The Plaintiff was familiar with the detention process and had been incarcerated on
numerous previous occasions without making similar claims against the Defendant,
so it is implausible that this detention would uniquely cause mental and emotional
distress, physical pain and anguish.
9. As such, the Defendant contends that the Claim against the Defendant should be
dismissed with costs awarded to the Defendant.
10. In a Reply filed on 12th November 2024, the Plaintiff denies that he had ever shown any
propensity to be violent or to try to escape, and points out that he was at a police station
with many police officers and was in an enclosed room. For the first time, the Plaintiff
makes reference to being tasered by the police, pleading the following:
Paragraph 5: “…Furthermore, the tasering of the Plaintiff was a very painful experience and
unnecessary. It was also unlawful.”
Paragraph 8: “…The whole tasering, arrest, and detention was unlawful. Aggravated damages,
punitive damages etc. should be awarded.”
4

11. The trial of this matter was held virtually on Thursday 6th November 2025 and Friday 7th
November 2025.
12. The only witness statement filed on behalf of the Plaintiff was that of the Plaintiff himself.
13. On behalf of the Defendant, the following two police officers gave witness statements:
i. Emeial Shepherd.
ii. Hussien Higgs.
THE EVIDENCE
PLAINTIFF’S WITNESS
a. Cedric Simms
14. The only witness for the Plaintiff was the Plaintiff himself who gave a short witness
statement comprising twenty-four (24) lines that essentially repeated what was pleaded
in his Statement of Claim, with one interesting addition. He began his statement by
saying: “On the 4th August 2022 I was chased by the police in Grand Turk and tasered
unlawfully and arrested unlawfully.” (Emphasis mine).
15. He went on to state: “The assault by the police with the taser was blatantly unlawful and
contrary to the police’s policy on the use of the taser, and the arrest was unlawful and
contrary to section 22(3)(a) and (b) of the DVO 2025.”
16. The Plaintiff has therefore, in his witness statement, stated that he was tasered and is
seeking damages for it.
5

17. At the trial, the Plaintiff did not seek to amplify his witness statement prior to being cross-
examined by counsel for the Defendant, Ms. Khadija McFarlane.
18. I have extracted what I consider to be important parts of the cross-examination of the
Plaintiff in which he responds to matters which were put to him by counsel for the
Defendant:
Put: You were upset and that was the reason why on 4th August 2022 you went to her place of
work.
Answer: Not true. I went to the supermarket. My wife works at the supermarket. She has her
times. It is Sunny Foods Supermarket. On 4th August 2022 when I went there, my wife was
working at that time.
Put: You were upset and started threatening your wife at her place of work.
Answer: It is not true that I started threatening my wife at her place of work. I was not upset.
Put: In threatening her, you told her you could make her lose her job.
Answer: I know nothing about that.
Put: You ran behind her at her place of work when she tried to get away.
Answer: I do not know what you call ‘ran’. I can’t recall that.
Put: You told your wife, “I want to see you sleep tonight.”
6

Ans: I do not know nothing about that.
Put: During your interactions with your wife she indicated to you she would call the police.
A: I know nothing about that.
Put: Your response was “F.. police’
A: I know nothing about that.
19. In response to questions by Ms. McFarlane concerning his arrest and whether he had
recognized the vehicle as a police vehicle, the Plaintiff went on to say, inter alia:
“…In my witness statement that I signed in relation to this matter, I told this court that police
officers arrested me on 4th August 2022. This was the same day that I was at my wife’s place of
work. Later on that same date I was driving my vehicle. No one tried to stop me. A car stopped in
front of me. I got out of my car and ran. No one told me they were police officers. The car that
stopped me, I saw the back of the vehicle. Having seen the back of it, I took no time to look to see if
it was a marked police vehicle. It is not true that the police vehicle had on its siren. I do not know
what vehicle it was. The vehicle blocked my vehicle. After my vehicle was blocked, I ran out of my
vehicle and fled. I do not know who it was that was behind me and whether there were police
shouting at me to stop. I did not know who it was behind me. I continued running. I find out after
that there were police officers behind me telling me to stop…”
20. The Plaintiff was then questioned about the portion of his witness statement in which he
had stated that he was tasered. His responses were, inter alia:
“…I fell down. The police put his feet on my neck and tasered me. I did not put this in my witness
statement. I accept I did not put it. That don’t mean it didn’t happen…. When the police caught
up to me they did not inform me that I was arrested for breach of an interim Protection Order. I
7

know that when I reached court. They just put me in a cell. They never informed me of that. They
tasered me in my back and put me in a cell…”
DEFENCE WITNESSES
a. Emeial Shepherd
21. The first Defence witness was Emeial Shepherd, who is a sergeant of police (Number 183)
with the Royal Turks and Caicos Islands Police Force and was, at the time of the incident,
stationed in Grand Turk, Turks and Caicos Islands.
22. In his Witness Statement, he said that on Thursday 4th August 2022, at around 7:30 p.m.,
he was on duty at the Grand Turk Police Station when a report was received from one
Guilene Fils Amie Simms that her husband, Cedric Simms, was at her place of work at
Sunny Foods Supermarket located on West Road, Grand Turk, harassing her and causing
a disturbance.
23. P.C. Shepherd stated that Police Constable Higgs and Police Constable Brown responded
to the report. Sometime after, they returned to the station, following which another report
came in stating that the Plaintiff had returned to his wife’s place of work. Sgt. Shepherd
stated that he was aware that there was an Interim Protection Order issued against the
Plaintiff because it was circulated amongst officers and a copy was placed on the notice
board of the police station. A copy of the Interim Protection Order was annexed to his
Witness Statement as “E.S.1”. It is instructive that one of the terms of the order is that the
Plaintiff is not to come within 100 feet of his wife, Guilene Fils Amie Simms.
8

24. According to Sgt. Shepherd, he, in company with Officers Marissa Brown and Hussien
Higgs, proceeded to the supermarket but did not see the Plaintiff on their arrival there.
However, one of the supermarket’s employees advised him that the Plaintiff’s wife had
locked herself in an office because she was in fear of the Plaintiff. Sgt. Shepherd then went
to the office and met Mrs. Simms who told her that the Plaintiff had returned to the
supermarket after the police had left the first time. Sgt. Shepherd was then shown CCTV
footage of Mr. Simms in the supermarket.
25. Later that day, Sgt. Shepherd was driving a marked police vehicle in the vicinity of
Hospital Road, where he observed the Plaintiff driving in the same area. Sgt. Shepherd
drove the vehicle to block the path of the Plaintiff’s vehicle. The Plaintiff then exited his
vehicle and ran. Sgt Shepherd and Police Constable Bido chased the Plaintiff, with Sgt.
Shepherd shouting behind him to stop. However, the Plaintiff continued running. Sgt.
Shepherd and Police Constable Bido apprehended the Plaintiff with the use of a taser. Sgt.
Shepherd stated that the taser was used because the Plaintiff refused to stop, it was a less
lethal option and the Plaintiff is known to be aggressive towards police officers.
26. Sgt. Shepherd stated that after apprehending the Plaintiff, he cautioned him and informed
him that he was under arrest for breach of a Protection Order issued by the Court, and the
Plaintiff was subsequently charged with breach of the Interim Protection Order. He
further stated that he had arrested the Plaintiff pursuant to the powers of arrest without
warrant under section 20 of the Domestic Violence Act.
27. Whilst acknowledging that he did not have a copy of an affidavit from Mrs. Simms before
making the arrest as required by section 22(3)(b) of the Domestic Violence Act, he
contended that he did interview her at her place of employment and that in any event any
affidavit would have been sworn to by her since she was the applicant in the domestic
violence proceedings.
9

28. Sgt. Shepherd went on to describe the conditions of the cell at the Grand Turk Police
Station in which the Plaintiff was housed. According to him, the holding cell is double
bunk and houses a toilet, and there are shower facilities along the inner corridor at the
end.
29. He added the following:
i. On Friday 5th August 2022, Police Constable Hussien Higgs went to the magistrate’s
court to make arrangements for the Plaintiff’s appearance but was informed that the
magistrate was not in office on that day. Later that day, the Plaintiff was given the
opportunity to meet with his attorney-at-law at the Grand Turk Police Station.
ii. The Plaintiff had previously been charged with other offences and was considered to
be at a high risk of escaping custody, hence the reason for him being placed in
handcuffs when meeting with his attorney-at-law. A copy of the Plaintiff’s Conviction
Sheet was annexed to his Witness Statement and marked “ES2”.
iii. P.C. Higgs went to the magistrate’s court on Monday 8th August 2022 to make
arrangements for the Plaintiff to appear, but was informed that a file needed to be
produced.
iv. On Wednesday 10th August 2022, P.C. Higgs took the Plaintiff to the Grand Turk
Magistrate’s Court where he appeared before the magistrate and was remanded into
custody until 31st August 2022.
v. During the time that the Plaintiff was in custody at the Grand Turk Police Station, he
requested certain meals that were not on the menu and they were provided to him.
The relevant Movement of Prisoner Sheets for the Plaintiff (which recorded meals,
smoking breaks, visits by attorneys and other persons, removal for medical attention
and court appearances etc.) were annexed and exhibited to his Witness Statement and
marked “E.S.3”.
30. Under cross-examination by Mr. Skippings, P.C. Shepherd’s responses included:
10

“…I was part of the process of when Mr. Simms was arrested. I did not taser him. I know who did
it. P.C. Lewis Bido did it. I was present at the time. When Mr. Simms was tasered, he was nowhere
near to the supermarket. A taser should be used by the police on someone in order to effect an arrest
where use of force is needed. I am aware of the Use of Force Police Policy about using the taser. I
am not aware that para 9.1 of the policy says a taser should only be used where there is violence or
threat of violence. I would have to look back at that policy…
…I was present when he was tasered. Just before he was tasered, he was escaping custody. He was
running. The taser was used in order to subdue him. He was tasered once. The taser is equipped
with two cartridges. He was tasered. He fell to the ground. Before he was tasered, we shouted to
him to stop by saying: “Stop! Stop!” I shouted nothing else. I shouted “Stop!” from the distance I
was. There was a 25 feet distance of him from me. The one who tasered him was closer to him. He
was running. I cannot recall everything that was said. Once he was tasered, P.C. Bido placed the
handcuffs on him and stand him up. We held him on the ground in order to put the handcuffs on.
I never placed my leg on his neck. He was laying on his back. We held his lower leg and arm and
placed the handcuffs on him. I cannot recall what part of the body P.C. Bido held.
I just gave instructions about what they should do. I gave them instructions to take him before the
magistrate’s court. He was supposed to go to court the following day. However the magistrate was
not there. We went to the magistrate’s court in Grand Turk. I spoke with the personnel at the court.
I do not remember who it was. I remember it was a female person. She said the magistrate was in
Provo from what I recall. She was not on island. I did not try the Chief Magistrate to see if he would
do it. I am not the investigating officer in this case. P.C. Higgs is the investigating officer. I was
part of the arresting team. I did not have an affidavit before arresting Mr. Simms. Mr. Simms was
not arrested at the scene of the supermarket. At the time Mr. Simms was arrested, he was not
engaging in conduct which amounted to physical violence. At the time Mr. Simms was arrested,
he was not attempting to engage in conduct which amounted to physical violence. He was running.
Mr. Simms was taken to the Grand Turk Police Station after his arrest. I cannot recall the date he
saw the magistrate…”
Hussien Higgs
11

31. The second and final Defence witness was Hussien Higgs. He is a Police Constable (No.
244) of the Royal Turks and Caicos Police Force, attached to the Operations and Response
Unit. He is the Investigating Officer in the matter.
32. In his Witness Statement, P.C. Higgs said that on Thursday 4th August 2022, he was on
duty at the Grand Turk Police Station along with Sergeant Emeial Shepherd, Police
Constable Marissa Brown and District Constable Lewis Bido. At around 6:30 p.m. he
received a telephone call via his cellular phone from one Mr. Rodney Dorsainvil, who was
employed as a security officer with the supermarket Sunny Foods located on West Road,
Grand Turk. Mr. Dorsainvil stated that a man known to him as Cedric Simms had come
to the supermarket and started an argument with his co-worker Guilene Fils Amie Simms
and was behaving aggressively, using obscene language and accusing him of having a
relationship with his wife. He reported that Mr. Simms also threatened to go for his gun
and kill him.
33. P.C. Higgs went on to say the following at paragraphs 4, 5 and 6 of his Witness Statement:
Paragraph 4:
“After receiving that call, Sergeant Shepherd and I responded in a marked police vehicle and went to
the Sunny Foods Supermarket to investigate the report. However, I did not see Mr. Simms when I
arrived at the supermarket. Around 7:00 p.m., I received another call from Guilene Fils Amie Simms,
who stated that her husband, Cedric Simms, came to her workplace, started an argument and chased
her through the aisle in the supermarket. She also reported to me that Mr. Simms told her that he is
going to make her lose her job. As a result of this second report, Sergeant Shepherd and I revisited the
scene and did not see Mr. Simms.”
Paragraph 5:
“I, along with other police officers, eventually saw Mr. Simms driving a silver Toyota Will Cypha on
Front Street. Constable Bido and I signaled at Mr. Simms with the use of siren and flashing lights to
stop his vehicle. Mr. Sims failed to acknowledge the police signal. Immediately, we began to pursue Mr.
12

Simms by chasing. An additional police vehicle joined the chase in pursuit of Mr. Simms, which
resulted in Mr. Simms’ vehicle being boxed in.”
Paragraph 6:
“Mr. Simms then fled from his vehicle and I, along with other officers, began a foot chase. I heard
Sergeant Shepherd shout at Mr. Simms to stop but Mr. Simms ignored the command and continued
to run from us. During the chase, I fell and as a result I did not see when Mr. Simms was subdued by
other officers as they followed him into nearby bushes. I was later informed by Sergeant Shepherd that
Mr. Simms had to be tasered in order to subdue him as he was fleeing from the police. I am aware that
the Use of Force Policy within the police force requires officers to use the least lethal option when trying
to apprehend someone. In the circumstances where Mr. Simms was running and refusing to follow the
commands to stop, the least lethal option would be the use of a taser.”
34. P.C. Higgs went on to say that the Plaintiff (Mr. Simms) was taken to Sgt. Shepherd who
informed the Plaintiff of the report made against him and cautioned him. The Plaintiff
was then arrested pursuant to the power of arrest without a warrant under section 20 of
the Domestic Violence Act and charged with breach of the Interim Protection Order.
35. P.C. Higgs recalls taking the Plaintiff to the hospital on Friday 5th August 2022 after the
Plaintiff had complained of back pain from being struck with the taser. At the hospital,
the Plaintiff was examined by one Dr. Kiravu, who advised that an x-ray could be
performed, but the Plaintiff declined the x-ray.
36. According to P.C. Higgs, the Plaintiff was taken to the Grand Turk Magistrate’s Court on
Friday 5th August 2022 but there was no court on that date because the magistrate did not
sit. He was again taken to court on the next available date, being Monday 8th August 2022,
but was told by the magistrate that a complete case file must now be prepared before the
matter could be tried, unlike in the past when the Information and a copy of the Interim
Protection Order would have sufficed.
13

37. P.C. Higgs stated that on Tuesday 9th August 2022, he caused a complete case file to be
prepared which included a summary of the facts of the matter, a statement from Mrs.
Simms, the Complaint and Charge Sheet. He stated that from his recollection he again
took the Plaintiff to court on Tuesday 9th August 2022, but was not certain of the date1.
However, the Plaintiff was remanded on this last court appearance.
38. P.C. Higgs also recalled escorting the Plaintiff out of his cell on occasion for him to smoke
and that the Plaintiff refused meals which were offered to him by the police.
39. Under cross-examination, P.C. Higgs stated, inter alia:
“…I agree that Mr. Simms was tasered on his back while he was running away. I said I took Mr.
Simms to the hospital. I took him out of the cells and also for smoke breaks and when he asked to
speak with his attorneys.
I took Mr. Simms to court. On 8th August 2022 I was told I need to prepare a complete file in order
for the matter to be tried. It was not Mr. Simms’ problem that the police did not have their files
prepared. I am the investigating officer in this matter.…
Sgt. Shepherd attempted to take him to court on 5th August but the magistrate was not there. On
5th August, the Court was closed. Sgt. Shepherd and I were in close proximity when he came to
check on the court. When Sgt. Shepherd and I went to the court and it was closed, we did not speak
with anybody. We just left. I was informed that the magistrate was in a different sitting in Provo.
I think we spoke with Officer Roberts by the court. I saw Roberts by the court. When I say the court
was closed, there was no one by the Court…
1 It is noteworthy that Sgt. Shepherd had stated that the Plaintiff was next taken to court on Wednesday 10th August 2022 and
that this is not in dispute.
14

…I agree that Mr. Simms was at the station from 4th August 2022 to 10th August 2022 before he
went before a magistrate…
When Mr. Simms was tasered he was not at Sunny Foods…”
40. Under re-examination, P.C. Higgs said, inter alia:
“…When I said I agreed that Mr. Simms was in custody from 4th to 10th August, I agreed. I took
him to court on 5th, 8th and 9th August…
...In relation to my witness statement, I said at paragraph 8 that there was no court on that day.
From what I recall the magistrate was not sitting. When I said the court was closed that is what I
meant. I had spoken with the officer at the court. The doors of the court were closed...
The taser is the less lethal option. It is permitted to be used when trying to arrest someone or subdue
him. That is why the taser was used...”
THE PLAINTIFF’S ARGUMENTS
41. The Plaintiff’s arguments can be summarized as follows:
i. The Plaintiff’s arrest was unlawful and contrary to section 22(3) of the Domestic
Violence Act in that a sworn affidavit from his wife was required in order to execute
the Interim Protection Order.
ii. There is no evidence that the Plaintiff was within 100 feet of his wife.
iii. While at the Grand Turk Police Station, the Plaintiff was placed in a ‘dank, stinking
cell’ (to use the words of the Plaintiff’s attorney-at-law in his submission) and placed
in handcuffs and shackles.
iv. The use of the taser on the Plaintiff was in contravention of section 9(1) of the
Operational Use of The Taser Manual of the Turks and Caicos Islands Police Force
which provides that the use of the taser as a tactical option is available to a police
officer who is faced with violence or a threat of violence. The Plaintiff was running
15

away from the police and there was no violence or threat of violence from the Plaintiff
to them.
v. The Plaintiff was kept in police custody for a period of in excess of 48 hours before he
was taken before a magistrate. This was in contravention of section 22(6)(b)(i) of the
Domestic Violence Act.
THE DEFENDANT’S ARGUMENTS
42. The Defendant’s arguments can be summarized as follows:
i. Section 20 of the Domestic Violence Act provides an independent power of arrest to a
police officer to arrest without a warrant a person at the scene of an incident of
domestic violence whom he reasonably suspects of having committed an offence
containing an element of violence. This provision applies independently of section
22(3) (which is applicable where a person is arrested under a warrant of arrest) and
empowered the police to act in this instance. In addition, section 20(2) gives the police
the authority to arrest without warrant pursuant to the Criminal Law Act and section
30 of the Police Force Act.
ii. There was no element of neglect or delay in the detention of the Plaintiff and any delay
was occasioned entirely by court availability and the magistrate’s procedural
directions.
iii. The alleged “taser assault” was not pleaded in the Statement of Case and it is
immaterial that it was included in the Statement of Issues. However, if the court
proceeds to consider this, it is submitted that the taser was deployed tactfully and
defensively rather than punitively or as a tool of compliance and the officers’ belief in
the necessity of force was both honest and objectively reasonable in the circumstances
as they appeared at the time.
iv. The use of handcuffs and leg shackles was lawful, necessary and consistent with
established policing practice since the Plaintiff was considered to be a high-risk
detainee who had fled from officers immediately before arrest and was known to the
police for other offences. The restraint used was necessary and proportionate to the
risk presented.
16

LAW AND ANALYSIS
The Law
Issue 1: Was the arrest lawful?
43. I begin by reminding myself that it is not my role to make a finding on what transpired at
Sunny Foods Supermarket but rather to examine the information that the police had in
their possession when effecting the arrest, since there is no evidence or suggestion that
the police had fabricated the information that they were acting on. This information can
be summarized as follows:
i. The Plaintiff had gone to the workplace of his wife at Sunny Foods Supermarket even
though there was an Interim Protection Order in place that he was to stay 100 feet
away from her.
ii. The Plaintiff, whilst at the supermarket, accused a security officer of having an
intimate relationship with his wife and threatened to go for his gun in order to kill
him.
iii. After visiting the supermarket and returning to the station, the police received another
report that the Plaintiff had returned to the supermarket, started an argument with his
wife and chased her through an aisle in the supermarket.
iv. Upon returning to the supermarket, the police were told that Mrs. Simms had locked
herself in the office because she was in fear of the Plaintiff. Sgt. Shepherd met with
Mrs. Simms in the office and she confirmed that Mr. Simms had indeed returned to
the supermarket and was shown CCTV footage of him being present.
44. The Plaintiff contends that his arrest was unlawful and contrary to section 22(3) of the
Domestic Violence Act in that a sworn affidavit from his wife was required in order to
execute the Interim Protection Order.
17

45. As previously stated, the Defendant relies on section 20 of the Domestic Violence Act
which provides an independent power of arrest to a police officer to arrest without a
warrant a person at the scene of an incident of domestic violence whom he reasonably
suspects of having committed an offence containing an element of violence. The
Defendant argues that this provision applies independently of section 22(3) (which is
applicable where a person is arrested under a warrant of arrest) and empowered the police
to act in this instance.
46. The Defendant further relies on section 20(2) which it contends gives the police the
authority to arrest without warrant pursuant to the Criminal Law Act2 and section 30 of
the Police Force Act3.
47. Sections 22(1), (2) and (3) of the Domestic Violence Act provides as follows:
Warrant of arrest and procedure upon arrest of respondent where Magistrate issues Interim
Protection Order
22. (1) In issuing an Interim Protection Order, the Magistrate may make an order—
(a) authorising the issue of a warrant for the arrest of the respondent in Form X set out in the
Schedule; and
(b) suspend the execution of such warrant, subject to compliance with any prohibition,
condition, obligation or order imposed pursuant to section 4.
(2) The warrant referred to in subsection (1) shall remain in force unless the Interim Protection
Order is set aside.
(3) A police officer shall—
2 Chapter 3:01
3 Chapter 18:01
18

(a) execute a warrant of arrest upon its production and receipt of an affidavit in Form
XI set out in the Schedule, wherein it is stated that the respondent has breached any
prohibition, condition or obligation or order imposed pursuant to section 5; or
(b) arrest the respondent upon receipt of an affidavit by the applicant in Form XI set out in the
Schedule, which form states that—
(i) a protection order has been issued;
(ii) a warrant of arrest for the respondent has been issued;
(iii) the warrant of arrest has been lost or destroyed; and
(iv) the respondent has breached any prohibition, condition, obligation or order imposed
pursuant to section 11. [Emphasis mine].
48. Sections 20(1) and (2) of the Domestic Violence Act provide as follows:
Police powers of arrest without warrant
20. (1) Subject to this section, a police officer may arrest a person at the scene of an incident of
domestic violence, without a warrant, whom he reasonably suspects of having committed an
offence containing an element of violence.
(2) For the avoidance of doubt, a police officer may act in pursuance to the provisions of the
Criminal Law Ordinance and Police Force Ordinance, where he has reasonable cause to believe
that a person is engaging in or attempting to engage in conduct which amounts to physical
violence, and failure to act immediately may result in serious physical injury or death.
49. Section 30 of the Police Force Act gives the police the power to arrest without a warrant
and provides:
19

30. Without prejudice to a power to arrest conferred upon a police officer by any other law, any
police officer may arrest without a warrant––
(a) any person found committing any offence punishable upon criminal information in the Supreme
Court or upon summary conviction;
(b) any person who within view of such officer is committing a breach of the peace, or
whom such officer has reasonable grounds to believe is about to renew a breach of the
peace;
(c) any person, if an arrestable offence has been committed and such officer has reasonable grounds
to believe that such person has committed it; or
(d) any person, if the police officer has reasonable grounds to believe that an arrestable offence has
been committed and that such person has committed it. [Emphasis mine].
50. Section 3 of the Criminal Law Act Chapter also gives the police the power to arrest
without a warrant and provides:
3. (1) The powers of summary arrest conferred by the following subsections shall apply to offences
for which the sentence is fixed by law or for which a person (not previously convicted) may under
or by virtue of any enactment be sentenced to imprisonment for a term of five years, and to attempts
to commit any such offence; and in this Ordinance and in any other law “arrestable offence” means
any such offence or attempt.
(2) Any person may arrest without a warrant anyone who is, or whom he, with reasonable cause,
suspects to be, in the act of committing an arrestable offence.
(3) Where an arrestable offence has been committed, any person may arrest without warrant anyone
who is, or whom he, with reasonable cause, suspects to be, guilty of the offence.
20

(4) Where a police officer, with reasonable cause, suspects that an arrestable offence has been
committed, he may arrest without warrant anyone whom he, with reasonable cause, suspects to be
guilty of the offence.
(5) A police officer may arrest without warrant any person who is, or whom he, with reasonable
cause, suspects to be, about to commit an arrestable offence.
(6) This section shall not affect the operation of any enactment restricting the institution of
proceedings for any offence, nor prejudice any power of arrest conferred by law apart from this
section.
Common law powers to arrest without a warrant
51. I hasten to add that a police officer also has the power under the common law to arrest
without a warrant where a breach of the peace has been committed and it was reasonably
believed that a renewal of it was threatened. In R v Howell4, Watkins LJ said, inter alia:
…We hold that there is power of arrest for breach of the peace where (1) a breach of the peace is
committed in the presence of the person making the arrest, or (2) the arrestor reasonably believes
that such a breach will be committed in the immediate future by the person arrested although he
has not yet committed any breach, or (3) where a breach has been committed and it is reasonably
believed that a renewal of it is threatened.
The public expects a policeman not only to apprehend the criminal but to do his best to prevent the
commission of crime, to keep the peace in other words. To deny him, therefore, the right to arrest a
person who he reasonably believes is about to breach the peace would be to disable him from
preventing that which might cause serious injury to someone or even to many people or to property.
The common law, we believe, whilst recognising that a wrongful arrest is a serious invasion of a
person's liberty, provides the police with this power in the public interest.
4 [1981] 3 All ER 383 at page 388
21

We entertain no doubt that a constable has a power of arrest where there is reasonable apprehension
of imminent danger of a breach of the peace; so for that matter has the ordinary citizen…”
52. In Howell5, a breach of the peace was defined by Watkins LJ6 in the following way:
“…We cannot accept that there can be a breach of the peace unless there has been an act done or
threatened to be done which either actually harms a person, or in his presence his property, or is
likely to cause such harm, or which puts someone in fear of such harm being done...”
Analysis
53. Firstly, it is clear to me that the arrest was effected by the police without a warrant and, in
the circumstances, the provisions of section 22 (including a requirement for there to be an
affidavit outlining the breach) are inapplicable.
54. The issue then is whether the police correctly exercised their power to arrest without a
warrant.
55. Looking at the position under statute, I begin with sections 20(1) and 20(2) of the Domestic
Violence Act which provide as follows:
20. (1) Subject to this section, a police officer may arrest a person at the scene of an incident of
domestic violence, without a warrant, whom he reasonably suspects of having committed an
offence containing an element of violence.
(2) For the avoidance of doubt, a police officer may act in pursuance to the provisions of the
Criminal Law Ordinance and Police Force Ordinance, where he has reasonable cause to believe
that a person is engaging in or attempting to engage in conduct which amounts to physical
violence, and failure to act immediately may result in serious physical injury or death.
5 Supra at paragraph 51
6 at page 389
22

56. I am of the view that the Defendant cannot rely on section 20(1) since the arrest was not
done at the scene of the incident (being the compound of the supermarket) but rather
some distance away.
57. I move on to consider the other provisions.
58. Section 3 of the Criminal Law Act also gives the police the power to arrest without a
warrant, but in circumstances where an arrestable offence has been committed. Section
3(1) defines an arrestable offence as one which is fixed by law or carries a term of
imprisonment of five years or more.
59. Section 16 of the Domestic Violence Act prescribes the maximum penalty for breach of a
Protection Order as being a fine not exceeding $10,000 or a term of imprisonment not
exceeding two years:
16. (1) Subject to subsection (2), a person against whom an order has been made and who—
(a) has had notice of the order; and
(b) contravenes any provision of the order or fails to comply with any direction of the Magistrate,
commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding $10,000 or to a
term of imprisonment not exceeding two years, or to both.
60. This would not qualify as an arrestable offence.
23

61. The other possible offences that were committed involved the threat to the security officer
and the chasing of Mrs. Simms in the supermarket. These would amount to assaults and
would also not qualify as arrestable offences.
62. I therefore form the view that the Defendant cannot rely on the provisions of the Criminal
Law Act.
63. I turn now to the Police Force Act and the common law.
64. Section 30(b) of the Police Force Act provides:
30. Without prejudice to a power to arrest conferred upon a police officer by
any other law, any police officer may arrest without a warrant––
(b) any person who within view of such officer is committing a breach of the peace, or whom such
officer has reasonable grounds to believe is about to renew a breach of the peace;
[Emphasis mine].
65. This somewhat codifies the common law position in Howell7 to the effect that a police officer
has the power under the common law to arrest without a warrant where a breach of the peace
has been committed and it was reasonably believed that a renewal of it was threatened.
7 Supra at paragraph 51
24

66. It would be recalled that a breach of the peace was defined by Watkins LJ in Howell8 as: ‘An
act done or threatened to be done, which either actually harms a person or, in his presence, his
property; or is likely to cause harm; or which puts someone in fear of such harm being done.’
67. I am of the view that the police had information which would have led them to form the
opinion that the Plaintiff was within 100 feet of his wife at Sunny Foods Supermarket since they
were told that the Plaintiff and his wife were inside the building and that he was at one point
arguing with her and at another point chasing her. I therefore do not accept the Plaintiff’s
submission that the absence of measurements to show that the Plaintiff was less than 100 feet
away from his wife would be fatal to the Defendant’s case in this particular matter (as opposed
to the criminal matter) in respect to an arrest for breach of the order. It would, in my view, given
the information available to the police (and in particular the fact that the Plaintiff had returned to
the supermarket and chased his wife), be unreasonable to expect the police to take measurements
or seek out a warrant when there was a likelihood that the Plaintiff would again imminently
return to the supermarket to pursue his wife. Based on the reports received, the police had
information that there was a breach of the Interim Protection Order and of the commission of
other offences involving the chasing of Mrs. Simms by her husband and the threat to the security
officer, as well as reasonable grounds to believe that the Defendant would return imminently to
the supermarket to harm his wife and the security officer.
68. In respect of the manner of the arrest, having assessed the testimonies of the witnesses, I find
the testimony of the Plaintiff (to the effect that he did not know that he was being pursued by
police officers) to be implausible. I accept, on a balance of probabilities, the evidence of the
Defence witnesses that the police were driving in marked police vehicles and signaled to the
Plaintiff with the use of siren and flashing lights to stop his vehicle. I find that the Plaintiff knew
that he was being pursued by police officers and chose to attempt to evade them.
8 Supra at paragraph 51
25

69. In Christie v Leachinsky9, Viscount Simonds said10, inter alia:
“…If a policeman arrests without warrant on reasonable suspicion of felony, or of other crime of a sort
which does not require a warrant, he must in ordinary circumstances inform the person arrested of the true
ground of arrest. He is not entitled to keep the reason to himself or to give a reason which is not the true
reason. In other words, a citizen is entitled to know on what charge or on suspicion of what crime he is
seized. If the citizen is not so informed, but is nevertheless seized, the policeman, apart from certain
exceptions, is liable for false imprisonment. The requirement that the person arrested should be informed of
the reason why he is seized naturally does not exist if the circumstances are such that he must know the
general nature of the alleged offence for which he is detained. The requirement that he should be so informed
does not mean that technical or precise language need be used. The matter is a matter of substance, and
turns on the elementary proposition that in this country a person is, prima facie, entitled to his freedom
and is only required to submit to restraint on his freedom if he knows in substance the reason why it is
claimed that this restraint should be imposed. The person arrested cannot complain that he has not
been supplied with the above information as and when he should be, if he himself produces the
situation which makes it practically impossible to inform him, eg, by immediate counter-attack
or by running away…” [Emphasis mine].
70. As such, where a police officer is effecting an arrest without a warrant in a situation in which
he is entitled to, the legality of the arrest will not be affected by the failure to inform the person
being arrested of the reason for his arrest if that person creates a state of affairs that makes it
practically impossible to inform him, such as by running away. Given the circumstances of the
arrest where the Plaintiff was fleeing from police officers, it would be reasonable to expect that
the police officers would have been unable to inform the Plaintiff of the reason for his arrest at
that time.
71. Taking everything into account, I consider the arrest to be lawful both under the common law
and the provisions of the Police Force Act.
9 [1947] 1 All ER 567, HL
10 at page 572-573
26

Issue 2: Was the Plaintiff Falsely Imprisoned and, if so, for what period?
72. Based on the evidence, the Plaintiff would have been taken into custody on the night of
Thursday 4th August 2022, sometime after 7:30 p.m.
73. The permissible 48-hour period of detention would have ended on the night of Saturday 6th
August 2022. He was taken to Court for the second time on Monday 8th August 2022, which would
have been the first court date at the expiration of the 48-hour period, it having expired during the
weekend. Having regard to this as well as the attempt by the police to take him before a
magistrate on Friday 5th August 2022 and the unrebutted evidence that there was no magistrate
available, I hold that the Plaintiff cannot succeed in a claim for false imprisonment of his detention
over the weekend of 6th and 7th August 2022.
74. The issue then relates to his detention over the period Monday 8th August 2022 to Wednesday
10th August 2022. In Regina (Lumba) v Secretary of State for the Home Department (JUSTICE
and another intervening); Same v Same (No 2) (Same intervening); Regina (Mighty) v Same (Same
intervening)11, Lord Dyson JJSC said12, inter alia, in relation to the tort of false imprisonment:
“…All that a claimant has to prove in order to establish false imprisonment is that he was directly
and intentionally imprisoned by the defendant, whereupon the burden shifts to the defendant to
show that there was lawful justification for doing so…”
11 [2012] 1 AC 245
12 at page 274 (paragraph 65)
27

75. There is no dispute that the Plaintiff’s detention continued over the period Monday 8th August
2022 to Wednesday 10th August 2022. The burden is on the Defendant to establish that there was
lawful justification for this period of detention.
76. It is clear from the evidence on behalf of the Defendant that when taken to court on Monday
8th August 2022, the Plaintiff could not be brought before the magistrate because the police were
not prepared, in that they did not have a proper case file in place. This was clearly no fault of the
Plaintiff. Quite remarkably, the Plaintiff was not brought back to court later that day or even the
following day but rather two days later on Wednesday 10th August 2022. No proper explanation
has been forthcoming from any of the Defence witnesses about why it took two days for the file
to be put in order.
77. The Defendant has correctly referred this court to Dallison v Caffrey13 where it was held by
the Court of Appeal that although the initial detention was lawful, continued detention could
become unlawful if it exceeded what was reasonably necessary for investigation or court
production. Lord Denning LJ said, inter alia, at page 370:
“When a man is lawfully arrested upon a charge, the onus lies on him to prove that he was kept too
long in custody. But if the period is prima facie too long, the onus shifts to the defendant to justify
it.”
78. I hold that the period of detention from the time the magistrate informed the Plaintiff that the
case file was required on Monday 8th August 2022 to the time he was remanded on Wednesday
10th August 2022 has not been justified by the Defendant and the Plaintiff is entitled to damages
for this period of false imprisonment.
13 [1965] 1 QB 348 (CA)
28

79. In respect to the prison conditions, on a balance of probabilities, I accept the evidence of the
Defence witnesses that the prison conditions were satisfactory and that the Defendant was
provided with meals and given the opportunity to shower. In arriving at this conclusion, apart
from the assessment of the testimonies of the witnesses, I had regard to the Movement of Prisoner
Sheets for the Plaintiff (which recorded meals, smoking breaks, visits by attorneys and other
persons, removal for medical attention and court appearances etc.) and which was annexed and
exhibited to the Witness Statement of Sgt. Shepherd and marked “E.S.3”. It notes the Plaintiff
being given and accepting meals and medication, being allowed to smoke, shower, make a phone
call and being allowed to speak with his attorney-at-law. The credibility of these records has in
no way been undermined by the Plaintiff’s attorney-at-law under cross-examination.
80. In all of the circumstances, the Plaintiff has not established a case for aggravated
damages.
81. The Plaintiff is therefore not entitled to any aggravated damages for the period of his false
imprisonment.
Issue 3: Is the Defendant liable for Assault and Battery?
82. I have accepted the evidence of the Defence that the Plaintiff was arrested when running away
from the police. I have also found that the Plaintiff knew that he was being chased by police
officers but still attempted to elude them.
83. The Plaintiff was known to the police, and annexed to Sgt. Shepherd’s Witnesss Statement
was a Conviction Sheet (Court Exhibit ES2) which was not challenged by the Plaintiff for
authenticity or contents. In these circumstances, I do not think that it would be unreasonable for
a prisoner with a history of convictions for offences and who was attempting to evade capture,
29

to be handcuffed and placed in shackles when taken out of the cell in order to prevent his escape
and for the protection of personnel at the police station and his attorney-at-law. I accept that the
Plaintiff was only placed in handcuffs and shackles when he was taken out of prison in order to
prevent him from escaping.
85. I turn now to the use of the taser. It would be recalled that the issue of the Plaintiff being
tasered arose for the first time (and very briefly at that) in the Reply and the Plaintiff’s Witness
Statement.
86. The Defendant has submitted that this should not be considered by the court because it was
not pleaded in the Statement of Claim.
87. In support of this, counsel referred to the principles outlined in Blay v Pollard 14 and
McPhilemy v Times Newspapers15.
86. I am in agreement with the Defendant that the paucity of the pleadings haunt the Plaintiff
here.
87. The principle that a court should not base its decision on issues which have not been properly
pleaded was underscored in Jacobs v Chalcot Crescent (Management) Company Ltd16 where Mr.
Justice Fancourt of the Chancery Division said, at paragraph 57:
“Further, where an issue has clearly not been pleaded and was not relied on at the start of the trial,
I consider that the onus lies as much on counsel for the party seeking to rely on it as on their
opponent to raise the matter with the judge and seek permission to amend. For the reasons given
14 [1930] 1 KB 628 at 634
15 3 All ER 775 at 792-793
16 [2024] EWHC 259 (Ch)
30

by Dyson LJ in Al-Medenni v Mars, a party is entitled to rely on the pleaded case as defining the
ambit of the issues to be decided at trial.”
88. Before proceeding, I think it is appropriate to make some general remarks on the use of the
taser by the police.
89. While the taser is a less lethal weapon than a firearm, it is still a lethal weapon that can
temporarily incapacitate someone, and its use must be reasonable and proportionate to the threat
faced. The use of a taser against a suspect on his back while running away can only be justified
in exceptional circumstances, such as where there is a reasonable belief that if the suspect is not
apprehended there is a threat of immediate serious harm to either the police or civilians. In
addition, prior to the taser being used, a warning should be given.
90. From the evidence, it would appear that the use of the taser was unjustified and amounted to
the use of excessive force because there is no evidence that a warning for the firing of the taser
was given or that there was an immediate danger of violence to anyone. However, I repeat that
this observation is based on the evidence before the court. In fairness to the Defence, the Plaintiff
never pleaded the use of a taser as a particular of either his complaint of unlawful arrest or assault.
It was raised for the first time in his Reply and then mentioned in his Witness Statement, and in
both places rather scantily and lacking in specifics. Perhaps if it had been properly pleaded the
Defence would have provided more details in its evidence about the manner of the tasering.
However, the matter having not been raised at all in the Statement of Claim, it would be
unreasonable to expect the Defence to address this in extensive detail in its evidence. The fact
that it was an Agreed Issue does not assist the Plaintiff.
91. In addition to there being no evidence of the duration of the tasering or whether it continued
while the Plaintiff was on the ground, I note the following:
31

i. There was information that the Plaintiff had breached an Interim Protection Order.
ii. He ran from the police despite repeated shouts for him to stop.
iii. The police had information of serious threats made by the Plaintiff to kill.
iv. He had gone to the supermarket and returned.
v. He was well known to the police with previous convictions.
92. The tasering arose for the first time in the Reply by way of passing reference and, while the
Defendant accepts in its witness statements that the Plaintiff was tasered, even if I had found that
the tasering was unlawful, I cannot grant the Plaintiff any relief for this either for the claim for
wrongful arrest or assault because it was not pleaded in his Statement of Claim and no application
was made on behalf of the Plaintiff to amend his Statement of Claim.
93. Indeed, the assault and battery were not particularized and, even though there is evidence
that the Plaintiff was taken to the hospital for treatment, no attempt was made by him to provide
any medical evidence of injuries sustained.
94. In the circumstances, the Plaintiff’s claim for damages for assault fails.
95. The decision of the Court is therefore as follows:
a. The Plaintiff’s claims for damages for unlawful arrest, assault and battery are dismissed.
b. The Plaintiff claim for damages for False Imprisonment for the period 4th August 2022 to
7th August 2022 is dismissed.
32

c. The Plaintiff is entitled to damages for False Imprisonment for the period 8th August 2022
(when he was taken to court) to 10th August 2022 (when he was remanded in custody) to
be assessed by the Registrar in default of agreement.
COSTS
96. Following the delivery of its decision, the Court invited the parties to address it on the issue
of costs.
97. On behalf of the Plaintiff, Mr. Skippings submitted that his client was partly successful and
should be awarded at least fifty percent of his costs.
98. Ms. Clemar Hippolyte addressed the Court on behalf of the Defendant on this issue. She
submitted as follows:
a. The Defendant has won on all but one issue.
b. The Court is bound to find that the Defendant was successful overall.
c. The Plaintiff should therefore pay the Defendant’s costs.
99. The Court then adjourned the matter in order to afford the parties an opportunity to make
further submissions on this issue in writing. Ms. Hippolyte availed herself of this opportunity by
elaborating on her oral submissions in a written submission filed on 18th December 2026.
100. Ms. Hippolyte submitted that the fact that the Defendant was unsuccessful on one issue is
no justification for it to not be awarded its costs. She referenced a number of authorities, including
Evelyn Julia Frith And Scotiabank (Bahamas) Limited17 where Isaacs JA reaffirmed that it is
usual for the successful party to be awarded its costs and that there is no automatic rule requiring
a reduction merely because that party has lost on one or more issues. She further submitted that
17 SCCivApp. No. 88 of 2021 at page 4 (paragraph 11)
33

a departure from this general rule requires special circumstances, such as a clear finding that an
issue was pursued unreasonably and that absent such a finding, the discretion should not be
exercised to deprive the successful party of its costs.
101. She also submitted as follows:
a. The Plaintiff’s success was confined to a narrow finding that a detention which was lawful
at its inception became unlawful for a limited period of two days. That finding does not
justify an issue-based costs order. It was not a separate or distinct issue, nor did it
materially increase the length or costs of the proceedings. It did not alter the overall
character of the case, which was driven by serious allegations of illegality and misconduct
that were comprehensively rejected.
b. On the contrary, the Court should have regard to the manner in which the issues were
pursued. The pleadings were wholly defective in relation to the alleged use of a taser.
Despite the absence of proper particulars or a sound factual foundation, that allegation
consumed a disproportionate amount of time and resources and required the Defendant
to prepare comprehensive submissions. As is clear from the judgment, the Court accepted
the Defendant’s submissions on that issue in full. The costs associated with that issue were
not self-inflicted by the Defendant but were occasioned by the Plaintiff’s defective
pleadings and conduct of the case. This further underscores why it would be unjust to
deprive the Defendant of its costs or to award costs to the Plaintiff on an issue-by-issue
basis.
102. The Plaintiff was unsuccessful in his claims for False Arrest, and Assault and Battery and
only partially successful in his claim for False Imprisonment. The Defendant is therefore entitled
to its costs. However, I find the case advanced by the Defendant to justify the Plaintiff’s detention
from 8th August 2022 to 10th August 2022 to be completely devoid of merit and unreasonably
pursued. In essence, the reason for this further detention was because the police had gone to court
woefully unprepared, and it took them a remarkable two further days to prepare the case file
before the Plaintiff could get an audience before a magistrate. In these circumstances, the award
34

of costs will be reduced to reflect this finding. I consider a reduction in the award of costs by one
quarter to be appropriate in these circumstances.
103. The Defendant shall therefore be entitled to three quarters of its costs to be assessed by the
Registrar in default of agreement.
DECISION OF THE COURT
104. The Order of the Court is as follows:
a. The Plaintiff’s claims for damages for unlawful arrest, assault and battery are dismissed.
b. The Plaintiff claim for damages for False Imprisonment for the period 4th August 2022 to
7th August 2022 is dismissed.
c. The Plaintiff is entitled to damages for False Imprisonment for the period 8th August 2022
(when he was taken to court) to 10th August 2022 (when he was remanded in custody) to
be assessed by the Registrar in default of agreement.
d. The Plaintiff shall pay three quarters of the costs incurred by the Defendant to be
assessed by the Registrar in default of agreement.
Dated 27th February, 2026
The Honourable Justice Chris Selochan
Judge of the Supreme Court
35
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Arrest was lawful under police/common law powers; plaintiff entitled to damages only for unlawful detention from 8 to 10 August 2022.
